response: blank The living room function $L(t)$ is derived by subtracting natural light $N(t)$ from the target 500 lumens, yielding $L(t) = 200 - 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)$ for times when natural light is insufficient, and zero otherwise. blank Since natural light peaks at 500 lumens at $t=6$, LEDs are only active when $N(t) < 500$, which occurs for $t \in [0, 6)$ and $(18, 24]$. blank Integrating this active output over the 24-hour cycle and multiplying by the 0.1 watts per lumen-hour rate results in a total energy consumption of 1200 watt-hours. blank The bedroom RGB combinations must satisfy the linear equation $0.3r + 0.6g + 0.1b = 500$ within the integer bounds of 0 to 255 for each channel. blank This equation defines a planar slice through the three-dimensional color cube, meaning infinite integer solutions exist as long as the sum of weighted intensities equals exactly 500. blank Valid sets include configurations where green is maximized to reduce the burden on red and blue, or balanced mixes where all three contribute proportionally. blank Any specific triplet $(r, g, b)$ is valid provided the calculated lumen sum matches the target and no single intensity exceeds the 255 unit limit. blank The homeowner can select any point on this plane to create unique visual patterns while maintaining the required 500 lumens of brightness.
